# Bug Bounty Hunting Runbook
## A Reproducible Process for Finding and Reporting Vulnerabilities

**Version:** 1.0
**Author:** Generated by Aster for Mattia
**Last Updated:** 2026-05-11
**Estimated Time to First Bounty:** 2-4 weeks (part-time)

---

## Phase 0: Setup (Day 1)

### 0.1 Create Accounts
Register on these platforms (in order of priority):

1. **HackerOne** — https://hackerone.com
   - Complete profile: photo, bio, skills, languages
   - Verify email and enable 2FA
   - Add payment method (PayPal or bank)

2. **Intigriti** — https://intigriti.com (EU-based, less competition)
   - Same profile completion
   - European platform = GDPR-aware programs

3. **Bugcrowd** — https://bugcrowd.com
   - Third largest, good variety of programs

### 0.2 Install Tools

```bash
# Core recon tools
sudo apt install -y nmap nikto sqlmap

# Web app testing
# Burp Suite Community: https://portswigger.net/burp/communitydownload
# OWASP ZAP: https://zaproxy.org/download/

# Subdomain enumeration
go install github.com/projectdiscovery/subfinder/v2/cmd/subfinder@latest
go install github.com/tomnomnom/assetfinder@latest

# HTTP probing
go install github.com/projectdiscovery/httpx/cmd/httpx@latest

# Directory brute-forcing
sudo apt install -y gobuster ffuf

# JavaScript analysis
npm install -g linkfinder
pip install jsbeautifier secretfinder

# nuclei - vulnerability scanner
go install github.com/projectdiscovery/nuclei/v3/cmd/nuclei@latest
nuclei -update-templates
```

### 0.3 Browser Extensions
- **FoxyProxy** — proxy switching for Burp
- **Wappalyzer** — technology fingerprinting
- **HackTools** — XSS/SQLi payloads
- **Retire.js** — vulnerable JS detection

---

## Phase 1: Target Selection (Day 1-2)

### 1.1 Choose Your First Program
**Selection criteria (all must be met):**
- [ ] Scope includes web application (not just mobile/API)
- [ ] Program has been active for 30+ days (not brand new)
- [ ] Has fewer than 50 other researchers (check reports/responses)
- [ ] Pays at least $150 per valid finding
- [ ] Clear, written scope document
- [ ] Company is in a sector you understand (IT/sysadmin = great fit)

**Best beginner categories:**
- SaaS products (complex functionality = more bugs)
- Fintech (higher payouts, well-defined scope)
- E-commerce (payment flows, auth, cart logic)
- Government/EU programs (Intigriti often has these)

### 1.2 Recon Checklist
Run this for every new target:

```bash
# 1. Subdomain enumeration
subfinder -d target.com -o subs.txt
assetfinder --subs-only target.com >> subs.txt
sort -u subs.txt > subs_unique.txt

# 2. Probe for live hosts
cat subs_unique.txt | httpx -status-code -title -tech-detect -o live.txt

# 3. Port scan (top 1000 ports)
nmap -iL subs_unique.txt -T4 --top-ports 1000 -oX nmap_results.xml

# 4. Directory discovery
gobuster dir -u https://target.com -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -o dirs.txt

# 5. Technology fingerprint
whatweb https://target.com

# 6. JavaScript analysis
python3 LinkFinder.py -i https://target.com -o cli > js_endpoints.txt
```

---

## Phase 2: Vulnerability Hunting (Day 3-21)

### 2.1 High-Impact Bug Classes (ordered by payout probability)

#### A. Broken Access Control (IDOR) — $200-5,000+
**Most common finding. Most likely to pay.**

Test pattern:
1. Log in as User A, perform an action (view profile, order, etc.)
2. Capture the request in Burp
3. Change the user ID / resource ID to User B's
4. If you see User B's data = IDOR confirmed

```
# Example
GET /api/users/123/profile  →  Your data
GET /api/users/456/profile  →  Someone else's data  ← VULN
```

**Where to look:**
- API endpoints with numeric/guessable IDs
- File download endpoints (invoice.pdf?id=123)
- Account settings pages
- Order/invoice history

#### B. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — $150-3,000
Test every input field with:
```
<script>alert('XSS')</script>
"><img src=x onerror=alert('XSS')>
javascript:alert('XSS')
```

**Priority targets:**
- Search fields
- Profile fields (name, bio, address)
- File upload filenames
- URL parameters that get reflected

#### C. Authentication Bugs — $500-10,000+
- Password reset token reuse
- Missing rate limiting on login (brute force)
- JWT manipulation (alg:none, weak secret)
- Session fixation
- OAuth misconfiguration

#### D. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — $500-5,000
- Any URL input field (webhook URLs, import from URL, avatar URL)
- Test: `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS metadata)
- Test: `http://127.0.0.1:8080/admin` (internal services)

#### E. Insecure Direct Object References in APIs — $200-2,000
- Modify API parameters: `?user_id=`, `?org_id=`, `?file_id=`
- Test both GET and POST/PUT/DELETE
- Check for missing authorization on admin endpoints

### 2.2 Testing Methodology (per endpoint)

For EVERY endpoint in scope, test:

1. **Authentication bypass** — access without login
2. **Authorization bypass** — access as wrong user
3. **Input validation** — XSS, SQLi, command injection
4. **Rate limiting** — brute force, enumeration
5. **Error handling** — verbose errors, stack traces
6. **Business logic** — negative quantities, price manipulation, race conditions

### 2.3 Daily Routine (2-3 hours)

| Time | Activity |
|------|----------|
| 0:00-0:30 | Recon: new subdomains, endpoints, parameters |
| 0:30-1:30 | Deep testing on promising endpoints |
| 1:30-2:00 | Test for access control issues across all features |
| 2:00-2:30 | Document findings, write reports |

---

## Phase 3: Report Writing (Same day as finding)

### 3.1 Report Template

```markdown
# [Vulnerability Type] in [Feature/Endpoint]

## Summary
One-line description of the vulnerability.

## Severity: [Critical/High/Medium/Low]
CVSS 3.1 Score: X.X (calculate at https://www.first.org/cvss/calculator/3.1)

## Affected Component
- URL: https://target.com/api/v1/endpoint
- Parameter: user_id
- Method: GET

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Log in as attacker@test.com (password: test123)
2. Navigate to https://target.com/profile
3. Intercept the GET /api/users/[ATTACKER_ID] request in Burp
4. Change the user_id parameter to [VICTIM_ID]
5. Observe that the response contains the victim's personal data

## Impact
An authenticated attacker can access any user's personal data 
including name, email, address, and payment information by 
modifying the user_id parameter. This affects all X users of 
the platform.

## Proof of Concept
[Screenshot showing the vulnerability with sensitive data redacted]

## Remediation
Implement server-side authorization checks to verify the 
authenticated user has permission to access the requested resource.
Do not rely on client-side access controls.
```

### 3.2 Report Quality Checklist
- [ ] Title is clear and specific
- [ ] Steps are reproducible by someone with no context
- [ ] Impact is quantified (number of users, data types)
- [ ] Screenshots show the actual vulnerability (redact PII)
- [ ] CVSS score is calculated and justified
- [ ] Remediation is specific, not generic
- [ ] No actual user data exposed in the report

---

## Phase 4: Follow-Up & Scaling

### 4.1 After Submission
- Respond to triage questions within 24 hours
- Provide additional PoC if requested
- Don't argue about severity — accept triage decisions gracefully
- If marked duplicate, ask if there's a related but different issue

### 4.2 Scaling Up
Once you have 2-3 accepted reports:
- Apply for private programs on HackerOne (better payouts, less competition)
- Specialize in 1-2 bug classes (become known for IDOR or auth bugs)
- Build automated recon pipelines
- Write tools/scripts to speed up testing

### 4.3 Income Expectations (realistic)

| Phase | Timeline | Expected Earnings |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| Learning | Month 1 | $0-200 |
| Getting traction | Month 2-3 | $200-1,000 |
| Consistent | Month 4-6 | $500-3,000/month |
| Experienced | Month 6+ | $1,000-10,000/month |

---

## Tools Quick Reference

| Tool | Purpose | Install |
|------|---------|---------|
| Burp Suite | Web proxy/scanner | portswigger.net |
| Nuclei | Vuln scanning | go install nuclei@latest |
| subfinder | Subdomain enum | go install subfinder@latest |
| httpx | HTTP probing | go install httpx@latest |
| ffuf | Fuzzing | go install ffuf@latest |
| nmap | Port scanning | apt install nmap |
| sqlmap | SQL injection | apt install sqlmap |
| ffuf | Directory brute | go install ffuf@latest |

---

## Ethical Rules (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

1. **ONLY test within the program's stated scope**
2. **Never exfiltrate more data than needed to prove the bug**
3. **Never access or modify other users' data beyond demonstration**
4. **Report immediately — don't chain bugs or delay disclosure**
5. **Respect rate limits — don't DoS the target**
6. **Never sell or share vulnerability details outside the program**
7. **Follow responsible disclosure timelines (90 days standard)**

---

## Mattia-Specific Advantages

Your background gives you real edges:

1. **Windows Server expertise** — Many bug bounty targets run Windows. You understand AD, Kerberos, IIS, SMB natively.
2. **Network knowledge** — You can spot misconfigurations others miss (firewall rules, DNS, VPN endpoints).
3. **Hyper-V experience** — Cloud providers often use virtualization. Understanding VM escape vectors is rare.
4. **CommVault/enterprise tools** — Enterprise backup software = high-value targets with less scrutiny.
5. **AI agent (me)** — Use me to analyze code, generate payloads, review reports, and automate recon.
